paragraphs.body: November 5th 2024
Fate Has a Twisted Type of Humor
March 2024 - Seattle Airport

Veronica hated flying. She hated traveling alone in general. With every year between present day and her parents’ murder, her fear of anything that reminded her of the days after felt more and more like a childish weakness needing vanquished than a reasonable reaction. But memory doesn’t listen to reason, it just finds reasons to replay those events over and over again, like that’ll bring them back.

annotations.text: With every year between present day and her parents’ murder
annotations.comment: The phrasing here is a little awkward, but I liked this opening.
I didn’t quite see the connection between lingering fear from the parents’ murder and traveling alone.

paragraphs.body: Feelings don’t listen to reason either, she reminded herself and her heart rate spiked higher and higher as she progressed through the TSA line. The x-ray machine couldn’t read the notebooks filled with newspaper clippings and notes cross hatching over each other so much that only Veronica would be able to read the detailed plans she would be carrying out over the next year. The metal detectors couldn’t read her mind and see the crimes she planned on committing. The pot bellied woman whose uniform was covered in the odd combination of Seattle Seahawks and Green Bay Packers enamel pins didn’t get paid enough to notice the shaking of Veronica's hands and she handed over her passport–wrinkled from one to many ferry trips to Canada–and answered the woman’s questions about why she was traveling to Korea.

annotations.comment: This was the more dynamic opening — she’s going to commit crimes!
Love this detail! I can’t tell at this point if Veronica is nervous because she hates traveling, is still processing her parents’ death, or is plotting to do bad things.

paragraphs.body: Veronica wasn’t technically lying when she said she’d gotten a job teaching English in Korea–the country’s school starting in March was too convenient with her taking an extra semester to graduate college–-but her freshman roommate, Kathleen, who was likely 2/3rds of the way through her first year of law school would have called it lying by omission.
June 2007 - Seattle Airport

It was Veronica’s first time in an airplane and she couldn’t be more excited. Her parents could barely corral her as she ran around the gates, dragging her Barbie suitcase behind her and proudly showing off the temporary tattoos that were only starting to fade from her kindergarten graduation two days prior. Anyone that politely oohed and aahed at the Penelope Frank tattoos was immediately regaled with every detail about her 6th birthday that would be happening in three days while Veronica would be in Seoul.

annotations.comment: Nice — I like the use of timestamps. I would suggest trying to keep your verbs as simple as possible. Just “while Veronica was in Seoul” works well here.

paragraphs.body: March 2024 - Yeongdeungpo District, Seoul

Veronica laid out her knives on her bed. She stared at her collection ranging from cheap switchblades to a handmade katana and began to inspect them. They were all she could think about from the minute she had triple-taped the boxes closed until she had them in her hands. She just felt so vulnerable without them.

annotations.comment: How did she get these knives to Korea?
annotations.context_left: Veronica laid out her knives on her bed. She stared at her collection rang
annotations.context_right: g from cheap switchblades to a handmade katana and began to inspect them. They were all she could th

paragraphs.body: Once she was satisfied that nothing had been damaged she turned her attention to the contents of her carry-on. She was careful to make sure the zipper didn’t get caught on anything as she opened the suitcase that was completely full of paper and a jumble of once color-coded ribbon. Ribbon–not yarn–because that made her feel too much like she was going insane. She unpacked the detailed maps of the various districts of Seoul. Different maps for every change made to the city in the last 17 years since Veronica had last been in Korea. She unpacked the stacks of floor plans for every building she could find of Yeongdeungpo, maps of just alleyways and every single street lamp and LED sign that could have made certain areas more or less visible. Right in the middle of the suitcase, in the most protected spot, was the sketch of the three people who murdered her parents.

annotations.comment: I’m not entirely sure I buy that she would bring the physical items instead of just having photos on her phone.

paragraphs.body: Tourists killed in Seoul’s murder capital late at night were so far down in the list of priorities that all the police did was sit six-year-old Veronica down with a sketch artist for 10 hours while she recounted every detail she could about the appearance of the assailants. The pessimist in Veronica said that they hadn’t cared enough to actually investigate–that no one cared but her–but the police had fully intended to look into it until photos of officer’s loved ones taken through sniper scopes with a polaroid of Veronica’s mother bleeding out with, 그만하고 해라, Korean for stop, written below it. Veronica dug through the bottom of the suitcase in search of three sticky notes that had once been different colors corresponding with the sexes of the murders but they’d all faded to a dusty yellow. Each had everything she could remember about each of them. Everything that the sketches couldn’t capture.

annotations.comment: A long time!
The officers? An officer?
Packing so much information into this sentence makes it harder to follow. I’d consider splitting it up into two sentences.

paragraphs.body: Sleep hadn't come easy to Veronica since her parents murder so the jet lag had yet to catch up to her. Between the flight and time in the apartment she’d been up for 36 hours by the time she had finished securing all of her research on the walls. The sketches above her desk so she would never forget the reason for coming back to Korea. She triple checked every door and window was locked and the three different alarms systems she had set up were primed. As if that wasn’t enough she dragged chairs under every door handle. Including the door to the closet she had chosen over the bedroom so that she could have more than just the external wall between her and any nonexistent intruders.

annotations.comment: Doesn’t that seem more dangerous than just having it on a device?
Does she own this building? How did she afford that?

paragraphs.body: June 2007 - Myeongdong District, Seoul

“Our tour will conclude here in Myeongdong District, which is known for being a very popular shopping spot for both locals and tourists . . .” the tour guide continued about the differences between Myeongdong and the ultra-wealthy Gangnam district they’d just been in, but Veronica could only listen to stuff she wasn’t that interested in before her eyes started to wonder to all of the different signs advertising the street food each stall specialized in, the various idol groups busking to the meager crowd of mostly students scurrying from regular school to cram school, and lastly her full attention to someone in a red panda costume dancing around a sign advertising a red panda cafe that was only three blocks from the hotel Veronica and her parents were staying in.

annotations.text: before her eyes started to wonder to all of the different signs advertising the street food each stall specialized in, the various idol groups busking to the meager crowd of mostly students scurrying from regular school to cram school, and lastly her full attention to someone in a red panda costume dancing around a sign advertising a red panda cafe that was only three blocks from the hotel Veronica and her parents were staying in.
annotations.comment: Another sentence full of interesting information but hard to follow because there’s so much of it.

paragraphs.body: Veronica knew better than to try and get her parents attention when she was supposed to be listening to the tour guide, but as soon as it had finished and her parents had unfruitfully attempted to tip the tour guide Veronica was dragging her mother by her skirt over to the panda man.

“Hello Miss,” the panda man said in broken English as he thrust bright orange fliers into Veronica’s parent’s hands. “Opening day is tomorrow! We offer a very good price, it’s only 15,ooo won* for the whole family per hour.” [*15,000 won = $11]

annotations.comment: I would include this information in the dialogue in some way.

paragraphs.body: “Mooomm!” Veronica half whined, half pleaded. “It would be the absolute best birthday present ever! Please please please.”

Veronica’s mother had just finished folding the orange flier into a perfect square to place into the mini outside pocket of her knockoff Marc Jacobs Stam bag that everyone Veronica’s mom’s age had bought last year when the tour guide ran up to them. Her face was a mix of fear and concern.

annotations.text: bought last year when the tour guide ran up to them
annotations.comment: Awkward phrasing

paragraphs.body: “I saw you talking to the man over there. I just wanted to make sure you guys were aware that new cafe is in a district we really don’t recommend tourists, especially foreigners, go.”
“It’s in Yeongdeungpo right? That’s where our hotel is.” Veronica’s dad interjected. “The map says the cafe is only a quarter mile from where we’re staying.”
The concern in the tour guide's face was replaced by shock, but the fear remained in her eyes.
“Even still, I just wanted to warn you. Please try to avoid being outside your hotel room when it’s dark out over there. I’d hate for something to happen to you.”

“We appreciate your concern, but I think we’re able to make our own decisions.” Veronica’s dad said, his tone oozing with the curtness he typically reserved for work phone calls that had gone on too long.

annotations.comment: I enjoyed this moment. I saw both characters clearly.

paragraphs.body: March 2024 - North Yeongdeungpo Middle School

As convenient as it was with Veronica’s December graduation, the idea of the first day of school being in March, and not September, felt wrong. There were people discussing the start of Korea’s cherry blossom season down in Jeju Island on Veronica’s train to work and the breeze was warm. Hell, even Lee Miyoung, North Yeongdeungpo Middle School’s principal and Veronica’s new boss, had given Veronica fresh flowers from her garden as a welcome gift.

The flowers were currently in one of those bougie square water bottles Veronica had grabbed from the vending machine in the teacher lounge. They were right next to a copy of the last picture of Veronica and her parents together. The original was kept in a maximum security safe hidden in the wall of her apartment. She couldn’t risk anything happening to it.

annotations.comment: Ah, okay, not a hotel. I forgot that she was moving there, not just visiting. How does she afford all of this security?

paragraphs.body: The last thing Veronica did before the students in her first period students came sprinting in the door right before the bell was to turn the picture away from the students. She didn’t need to explain all of that at 8:15 in the morning.

annotations.comment: Why would the picture be facing the students?

paragraphs.body: All of her students were about as nice as middle schoolers could be. The first years were wide eyed and balls of nervous energy. Without fail, every class, at least one of the first years, spilled the contents of their pencil case, scented highlighters and Santrito themed pens, scattering everywhere. It caused a massive commotion and plenty of snickers from those in the back. She'd likely have to fill out a mountain of paperwork as a result of their bullying sometime during the school year, but it was extremely useful.

Veronica learned a long time ago that you can learn more about someone based on how they react in a situation where there are no social consequences for doing nothing, or even reacting negatively, like someone dropping their pencils. Those who have recently experienced a similar event or immediate reaction was one of empathy will roll up their sleeves and begin to help. The faster they react the more that reaction stems from empathy; the more of a public negative reaction needed for them to react the more they are guided by past experiences. The former will always help if you ask, but the latter needs a reason to.

Those who do nothing, or simply freeze instead of reacting, are the bystanders. The first group makes the perfect witnesses. You could beat someone up until they drop dead and this group would simply walk on by while justifying in their mind. Those who freeze want to help but fear doing something to make it worse while others simply don’t care. This group won’t do anything unless specifically told what to do. They’ll walk past the beating but come forward when the police go on the 6 o’clock news asking for witnesses.

annotations.comment: I think all of this is interesting, but I wonder why it’s important for her to observe a bunch of middle schoolers for these characteristics? How does that tie to her parents?

paragraphs.body: Those who react negatively, whether that’s by snickering or snapping photos to add to the texts they are already sending their friends about it, are motivated by power and they achieve it by forcing others underneath them. Those who do it out of fear, crack the second they face someone who can truly take away their power without consequence. The big fish in the small pond can talk a lot of shit until it’s in the ocean. A gun makes a very effective ocean. Those who do it out of greed, however, have the highest potential. Potential to become the next Al Capone or president has yet to be determined, but the ones who look at every situation with nothing but the thought of how to make it benefit them the most were a rare and dangerous breed. A knife to the heart is the only way to handle them.

annotations.text: . A gun makes a very effective ocean.
annotations.comment: This metaphor doesn’t quite work. I wondered about how she could tell someone’s motivation without hearing their thoughts. Like, how would laughing at someone who drops their pencils look if the laugher felt greed?

paragraphs.body: Veronica couldn’t trust anyone, but she could trust people to act the way she categorized them. Just like she could trust that every one of her students would be telling everyone, including the other teachers, about their first foreigner teacher. However, what she couldn’t expect to happen was her to be popular because of it. That would be a slight wrench in her plans.

“I hear I may finally have some competition for favorite teacher this year,” a voice behind Veronica laughed as it spoke. Veronica’s hand instinctively reached for the rusted switchblade hung around her neck and hidden in her blouse before she stopped herself. It was rare for her to not hear people’s footprints approaching her.

annotations.comment: Wow.

paragraphs.body: “Oh! Sorry I didn’t mean to scare you. I’m Aria Yejun. I teach science a few doors down.”

Veronica couldn’t shake the feeling that she’d seen someone like him, but not him, before. Some part of her deep down was telling her to be cautious, but his smile was telling her she could relax. And afterall, her brain stopped being able to distinguish between harmful and harmless for years.

annotations.comment: I wondered how she was able to make such a complicated life change if she was dealing with trauma?

paragraphs.body: June 2007 - Yeongdeungpo Red Panda Cafe
Veronica was on a 6th-birthday-cake-fueled rampage. Her parents did their best to corral her, but the happy child fully armed with the but it’s my birthday argument was no match for them.

At the very least the cafe was empty except for them and the employees. A couple of local kids saw Veronica playing and asked their parents if they could join only for their parents to lock eyes with the owner and hurry away, promising to explain when the children got older. Veronica’s parents didn’t notice the wide berth everyone else in Yeongdeungpo seemed to give the cafe, they were too enamored by the bubble tea they were trying for the first time. Lost in their own sugar high.                                                                                                                                              The ___   The alarm on Veronica’s father’s phone went off. It was time to leave the cafe and head back to the hotel. At the sound of the alarm Veronica started to cry. She wasn’t ready to leave. She didn’t want her birthday to be over. So she decided to try the same tactic that got her the trip to the cafe in the first place.

annotations.comment: It’d be great to know a little bit more about why they’re in Korea. Are they Korean?
Can you tell us what the alarm was set for?

paragraphs.body: “Please, please, please! Can we stay pleeeeeease!” Veronica whined as she grabbed onto her fathers arm and started to swing. “Our hotel isn’t that far away! We don’t need it to be bright out to walk back!”
The cafe owner perked up at the mention of them walking back to their hotel as Veronica’s dad glanced at the sun setting between the mess of city streets.
“If the little lady would like to stay we offer 75% off for the last hour we’re open.” The store owner offered and that was what swayed Veronica’s parents to let her stay an extra hour.
The cafe employee and owner shared a sly smile at each other as the sun slipped below the horizon.
April 2024 - Yeongdeungpo District, Seoul

Veronica was getting tired of beating people up just for them to have no information. There were no official records of the red panda cafe she’d spent her 6th birthday at that she or the police could find, so she had to rely on her memory of the area around the cafe.

She’d spent most nights wandering around and targeting older people with the same gang tattoo that the red panda cafe workers had on their necks. Anyone old enough to have been an adult in 2007 was a target.
The knife Veronica had pressed against the sagging skin of the old man’s neck was just starting to draw blood when he finally relented.

annotations.comment: I find it kind of funny that she teaches by day and assaults people at night.

paragraphs.body: “FINE! Fine. I’ll tell you.” The old man held his hands up in the air and Veronica released some of the pressure on the knife. “The only Americans we killed was the ination for the 황제’s son, but they killed both of them. There weren’t any survivors let alone an American kid there.”
“How do you know?”
“The whole gang came to celebrate the next night. The 황제 only had one son, so it would be the only initiation until that boy grew up and had kids of his own.” [황제 = emperor/leader]
“What’s his name?” Veronica snarled.
“Who?
“The boy god dammit”
“I’d shoot my own children before I told you that.”

annotations.comment: Would someone like this have such excellent English? (Maybe!)

paragraphs.body: “That won’t be necessary. I’m more than happy to do that for you. Young-chul, you said your name was?”
That was the final nail in the coffin. Veronica now terrified the hitman.
“He’s getting ready to take over the gang, so he’s still living in Yeongdeungpo, okay?”
“Good enough,” Veronica released the old man, readjusted the hood and mask obscuring her face and slid the knife into the sheath strapped under her sleeve, before heading back out into the dark.

annotations.comment: This does have the feel of a comic book. It’s not uninteresting but it doesn’t feel realistic.

paragraphs.body: June 2007 - Yeongdeungpo District, Seoul

The darkness coated the ground like a sickness. The unusually overcast skies combined with the new moon made it so Veronica and her parents had to rely on the flickering street lamps in order to figure out how to get home.

annotations.comment: So, one note here is that you’ve set this scene up so much that we basically know what’s going to happen.

paragraphs.body: Veronica decided that now that she was six, she was too old to be afraid of the dark. Instead she focused on the sound of her parents grumbling about how they should have left the cafe when they had originally planned to and the cracks in the pavement they were walking on. She told herself the voices of the cafe workers followed by the sound of running was just her imagination.

After wandering for a couple of blocks in the direction Veronica’s father was absolutely sure was the direction of the hotel, Veronica’s father directed the family down an alleyway. He was completely certain that it was the street the hotel was on. However, it was just a deadend.

The cracks in the pavement grew thicker and more common as they approached where the road met the concrete wall that was likely the back of a restaurant. Besides the ally they had come down they were completely surrounded by highrises. Most of the windows had lights coming through it. A few even had shadows of families sitting down for dinner.
But none of them would ever come to help Veronica.
May 2024 - North Yeongdeungpo Middle School
Veronica froze when she entered her classroom. Sitting on her desk right next to the frame of her and her parents was a bunch of crocheted cherry blossom branches. The card next to them read:
Wow I can’t believe it’s now been two months since I first met you. I remember it so well.
On that day you had flowers on your desk, so I figured I’d get you some that would last forever.
I know how much you hate things that leave you too soon.
~ Yejun
And with that Veronica was grinning like an idiot.

annotations.comment: She’s also dating someone while having a full-time job and searching for her parents’ killers?

paragraphs.body:
She just stood there, running her fingers across the woven yarn over and over. Dumbfounded. No guy had ever asked her out, let alone bought her crocheted flowers after only dating for a month. No person had ever left her with no clue how to proceed and yet here was Yejun with his goofy smile that never seemed to leave his eyes even in the most serious of situations. 
	The rational part of her knew that dating Yejun was just opening a weakness, a distraction from the real reason she even came to Korea, but feelings have never listened to reason.

Veronica was still dumbfounded when the students for her first class of the day came rushing in, so the cherry blossoms were still sitting on her desk. It was all her students could focus on, so class became a giant guessing game for as long as they could keep asking questions in English.

annotations.comment: This is quite sweet.

paragraphs.body: Her students very quickly realized that the cherry blossoms were in fact a romantic gesture and that based on the way Veronica’s face flushed to match the bright pink of the blossoms’ center when asked if the one who sent the flowers was another teacher at the school, that it indeed was.

Thus, it took about five minute break students got between first and second period for the entire student body to know that Veronica, the school’s most popular teacher to both students and faculty, had a secret boyfriend–not that Veronica had ever though to use boyfriend to describe Yejun even though it was accurate–that was one of the teachers at North Yeongdeungpo Middle School.

annotations.comment: Her life is going pretty well!

paragraphs.body: By the end of the day when Yejun came to visit Veronica, the students had collectively come together to determine which teacher was most likely to be Veronica’s secret boyfriend and, entirely as a result of Yejun’s reaction to the rumor, Yejun was at the top of it.

annotations.comment: Is this guessing game important to the story?

paragraphs.body: June 2007 - Yeongdeungpo District
A little boy ran out from the entrance to the alleyway, crying and screaming for help. He couldn’t have been more than a year older than Veronica.
He’d been crying for some time. His face was red and swollen and there was snot dripping down his nose.
“Please help me . . .” The boy said through sobs, his whole body shaking with fear. “These people tried to kidnap me.”
“It’s okay sweetheart,” Veronica’s mother said and she knelt down to give the boy a hug. When the boy started to hyperventilate Veronica’s father and crouched down to be eye level with the boy.

“They’re coming for you.” He whispered. Veronica could barely hear it, but before she could blink she was running off to hide behind the dumpster over by the concrete wall. Veronica was too far away to hear, but right before the gang the little boy was being initiated into showed up.

annotations.comment: Wow! That’s upsetting.

paragraphs.body: The knife was lodged in Veronica’s father’s neck before he could plug in the final digit to call 119, the Korean equivalent of 911. The gang member who threw it could not have aimed any better. The knife hit one of the main arteries as it the heart pumped blood out of its four chambers into the rest of the body. From behind the dumpster Veronica could see a red goo shooting out of her father’s neck, like a fountain in one of the haunted mansions Veronica was never tall enough to ride. It took five minutes for Veronica’s father to be an inch from death. It took her mother a lot shorter.

annotations.text: It took her mother a lot shorter.
annotations.comment: Awkward. This seems like such a dramatic way to kill someone, and I’m not yet clear on why her parents were the targets.

paragraphs.body: Four gang members surrounded Veronica’s mother, each with a butcher’s knife in each hand. They created a twisted circle around Veronica’s mother, like a predator cornering its prey. Just like prey in the animal kingdom, Veronica’s mother was fighting a losing battle.
The gang members tossed the members around until the both of them had lost so much blood they could no longer stand. That’s when a rusty switchblade hung around the little boy’s neck.
“Now here’s the important part.” The little boy’s father stated. “With this you begin step one of learning everything you need to knock to take over for me.

The little boy looked terrified, but so would any child when facing this decision. Instead, however, the little boy chose the opposite decision from what most children would do: He grabbed hold of the switchblade and sliced across Veronica’s parents' necks.

Out of disgust with himself he throws the knife in the direction of Veronica as his father scopes him up in his arms and places him on his shoulders. The whole gang was more merry when they left after having brutally murdered two people then when they arrived. It was such a strange and traumatic sight that Veronica never forgot anything except for right before the gang heads out of earshot when the little boy’s mother calls out his name to congratulate him: Yejun.

annotations.comment: The brutality here makes me a little uneasy, given that this is not an American culture.

paragraphs.body: June 2024 -  North Yeongdeungpo Middle School

Yejun kissed Veronica for the first time on her 22nd birthday. It had been nearly three months since they had first met and well past two months since they had truly started dating, but the two of them were both too shy and worried about messing up what they had to try.

annotations.comment: She was hired when she was 21?

paragraphs.body: It was sweet and Veronica felt like if she could freeze time and live in one moment, she’d choose this one. She’d choose any moment she was with Yejun.
No matter what moment she chose, it was a hell of a lot better than the moments of her parents deaths she’d been stuck in for 17 years to the day that was for sure.

“Hey, so I know this is a really weird time to ask this, but today is a really big anniversary for my family.” Yejun said once the moment was over. “I hate celebrating it, but it’s a massive deal to the rest of my family and I thought maybe if they were meeting you for the first time it wouldn’t be so weird celebrating it.”

annotations.comment: Ah, I saw the irony here. He’s the little boy!

paragraphs.body: All of the awkwardness had returned to Yejun’s voice, but Veronica couldn’t care less. First kiss and now meeting the parents? She wasn’t sure if things were moving too fast or if she wanted them to move faster.
“Of course, I’ve got some anniversaries I'd rather forget, so I completely understand,” Veronica said, failing to admit that the anniversary she’d rather forget was also today.
“Whoo!” Yejun squealed as he picked her up and spun her around before kissing her again.
He then proceeded to carry Veronica out of his apartment and down to his car, but due to his scrawny stature, it was about as romantic as a weight lifting contest, but Veronica adored his effort.
June 2024 - Seoul Suburbs
When Veronica stepped outside of Yejin’s car she was suddenly aware of how plain her clothes were to everyone else arriving at the extravagant house.
“Are you sure I shouldn’t be wearing something more formal?” Veronica asked.

“You’re perfect as you are, my family just determined it was better to turn family events into a best dressed tournament instead of settling their problems like adults.” Yejun laughed as he said it, but Veronica could hear the resentment in his voice.

Somehow the concept of getting caught or possibly dying while torturing ex-gang members for information about her parent’s murder was less terrifying than meeting Yejun’s family, but she tried to find the courage she’d had every night on the streets of Yeongdeungpo searching for answers and kissed Yejun.
Both of them turned redder than a tomato, which caused both of them to burst out laughing at the sight and eased whatever tension asking about his relatives had brought to the surface.

Veronica froze as soon as both feet were in the door. Hanging right in front of her was a giant photo of Yejun and his parents and they looked exactly like how she had described them to the sketch artist.

Veronica had always imagined all of the anger that had consumed her since her parents death would fuel her when she confronted her parents' murders, but it wasn’t there. In fact it’d never been there when she was with Yejun. Instead of that fiery rage all Veronica felt as she turned to Yejun pain and confusion, which was matched in his eyes.
“You killed my parents.” she said as the tears began to fall.

annotations.comment: This is a really interesting story, Emily. I can tell you worked hard on it, and it’s effective. I thought you packed in a lot and while I did guess the ending, I was intrigued through much of it. I had trouble buying the more violet parts of the story, and the idea that Veronica was doing all of this in a foreign country at 21. I don’t think you really need her to be beating up random guys in the district — she could instead just be in Korea, trying to heal, perhaps, and maybe learn something about her parents, and then this situation emerges (although then, you still have the question of why Yejun is a gang leader AND a middle school Science teacher — teachers work pretty hard! It’d be hard to have that level of a hobby!). My suggestion here is that you rethink the comic book nature of some of this and ponder a more realistic version — what happens to this story when we have to believe all of it? How does Veronica go about her life when she’s not a super heroine? I think there’s an even more interesting story here. Nice draft! Happy revising!
